text.title: Decomposition Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Four common failure modes when splitting code, with examples and corrective rules.

antipattern.description: A single file (often named utils.py or helpers.ts) accumulating 1000+ lines of unrelated functions from across the domain.
antipattern.reason: No single developer owns it; it becomes a dumping ground; every feature touches it causing conflicts.

rule.statement: Split by domain: string_utils.py, date_utils.py, currency_utils.py — not one catch-all utils file.
rule.rationale: Domain-split utilities have clear ownership and a natural scope that prevents further growth.

antipattern.description: One function per file: add.py, subtract.py, multiply.py — each 3 lines.
antipattern.reason: Navigation overhead exceeds the cognitive saving; import chains become deep and fragile.

rule.statement: Set a minimum file size of 30–50 lines; group related operations into a single module.
rule.rationale: Files below 30 lines add import overhead without context savings; group by semantic cohesion.

antipattern.description: A service class receives an HTTP Request object and accesses request.query_params directly.
antipattern.reason: The service is now coupled to the HTTP framework; testing requires a real or mocked request, not plain data.

example.note: Service takes a plain user_id: int — framework-agnostic, testable with a simple integer.

antipattern.description: An index.ts that re-exports everything with export * from './UserService', './OrderService', etc.
antipattern.reason: Wildcard re-exports break tree-shaking; bundlers cannot remove unused exports, inflating bundle size.

example.note: Explicit named exports only; tree-shaker can eliminate unused symbols.
example.code: export { UserService } from './UserService'; export type { User } from './types';

rule.statement: Forbid export * in agent prompts; require explicit named exports.
rule.rationale: Agents generate barrel re-exports by default; the constraint must be in the prompt.

antipattern.description: Mechanical line-count splits that ignore semantic cohesion: user_part1.py / user_part2.py.
antipattern.reason: Arbitrary splits break logical units; require a responsibility-based target tree before any move.

antipattern.description: Agent uses cat old > new && rm old instead of git mv.
antipattern.reason: git blame and git log --follow break permanently; mandate git mv in the system prompt.

antipattern.description: Agent edits only the moved file and forgets to update call sites in sibling packages.
antipattern.reason: Cross-file refactors silently break imports; require grep-first + import-update + typecheck before committing.
